From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1948 British film by John E. Blakeley

| Somewhere in Politics | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Directed by | [John E. Blakeley](/wiki/John_E._Blakeley "John E. Blakeley") |
| Written by | Harry Jackson |
| Produced by | John E. Blakeley |
| Starring | [Frank Randle](/wiki/Frank_Randle "Frank Randle") [Tessie O'Shea](/wiki/Tessie_O%27Shea "Tessie O'Shea") [Josef Locke](/wiki/Josef_Locke "Josef Locke") |
| Cinematography | [Ernest Palmer](/wiki/Ernest_Palmer_(British_cinematographer) "Ernest Palmer (British cinematographer)") Ben R. Hart |
| Edited by | Dorothy Stimson |
| Music by | Fred Harries |
| Production company | [Mancunian Films](/wiki/Mancunian_Films "Mancunian Films") |
| Distributed by | Mancunian Films (U.K.) |
| Release date | * 19 November 1948 (1948-11-19) (London) |
| Running time | 108 minutes |
| Country | United Kingdom |
| Language | English |

***Somewhere in Politics*** (also known as ***A Full House***) is a 1948 British [comedy film](/wiki/Comedy_film "Comedy film") directed and produced by [John E. Blakeley](/wiki/John_E._Blakeley "John E. Blakeley") and starring [Frank Randle](/wiki/Frank_Randle "Frank Randle"), [Tessie O'Shea](/wiki/Tessie_O%27Shea "Tessie O'Shea") and [Josef Locke](/wiki/Josef_Locke "Josef Locke").[[1]](#cite_note-BFIsearch-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was written by Harry Jackson.

It was the fifth in the *Somewhere* series of films featuring Randle, and was followed by *[It's a Grand Life](/wiki/It%27s_a_Grand_Life "It's a Grand Life")* (1953).

According to the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") (BFI), only a 18-minute segment of the film is known to exist, which was released as a short film entitled *Full House* and released nearly 12 years later in June 1960.[[3]](#cite_note-bfimostwanted-3)

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Somewhere_in_Politics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Joe Smart is a radio repairman who enters the political arena competing in an upcoming election against his own boss. Joe wins the election, but encounters various troubles in the process.

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Somewhere_in_Politics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [Frank Randle](/wiki/Frank_Randle "Frank Randle") as Joe Smart
* [Tessie O'Shea](/wiki/Tessie_O%27Shea "Tessie O'Shea") as Daisy Smart
* [Josef Locke](/wiki/Josef_Locke "Josef Locke") as Cllr. Willoughby
* Sally Barnes as Marjorie Willoughby
* Syd Harrison as Tony Parker
* Max Harrison as Arthur Parker
* Bunty Meadows as Martha Parker
* [Jimmy Clitheroe](/wiki/Jimmy_Clitheroe "Jimmy Clitheroe") as Sonny
* [Sonny Burke](/wiki/Sonny_Burke "Sonny Burke") as Reggie Smart
* [Anthony Oakley](/wiki/Anthony_Oakley "Anthony Oakley") as Howard
* [Bernard Graham](/wiki/Bernard_Youens "Bernard Youens") as Bank Manager
* Effi McIntosh as Mrs. Jones
* Kay Compston as Lady Hazelmere
* Fred Simister as Detective Sergeant
* George Little as Mayor

## Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Somewhere_in_Politics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

*[The Monthly Film Bulletin](/wiki/The_Monthly_Film_Bulletin "The Monthly Film Bulletin")* wrote: "The provinces will probably enjoy this knockabout comedy, 'though many Londoners will fail to see the point. The pace is rapid and much helped by the efforts of the cast."[[4]](#cite_note-4)

*To-Day's Cinema* concluded: "In addition to the untiring inventiveness of the star in grimace and tumble, the escapades are served by the clowning of Tessie as Randle's electioneering wife as she also heaves her 'two-ton' around in abandoned dance as by the singing of sentimental ditty by Josef Locke, and by the lunacies of Syd and Max in song and sally. It is all put over with pace and vigour, and will doubtless register heartily with the legion of Randle fans."[[3]](#cite_note-bfimostwanted-3)
